name: Soria, Cubo de la Solana, and Almenar de Soria Loop
name_original: Cubo de la Solana y Cabrejas del Campo — circular desde Soria
description: This 61.9-mile moderate road cycling route from Soria explores the agricultural landscapes and Romanesque churches around Cubo de la Solana
region_intro: Road cycling around Pinares offers diverse and challenging terrain, characterized by varied topography that includes plains, gentle hills, and significant mountain passes. The region features extensive pine forests, well-maintained roads, and natural attractions like the Cuerda del Pozo Reservoir. Cyclists can navigate through areas such as the Sierra de Villafranca and the Parque Natural de Sierra Cebollera, providing a range of landscapes for different cycling preferences. Best road cycling routes around Pinares The most popular road cycling route is Rodeo a los Picos de Urbión — circular por Parque Natural Lagunas Glaciares de Neila, a 76.0 miles (122.4 km) trail that takes 6 hours 14 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the Picos de Urbión and the glacial lagoons of Neila Natural Park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Montenegro, Peña Hincada, and Santa Inés Passes Loop, a difficult 62.7 miles (100.9 km) path. This route features multiple challenging mountain passes, offering significant elevation gain. Local road cyclists also love the Laguna Negra de Urbión — circular desde Vinuesa, a 23.5 miles (37.8 km) trail leading through the scenic area around Laguna Negra de Urbión, often completed in about 2 hours 20 minutes. Road cycling around Pinares is defined by extensive pine forests, challenging mountain passes, and tranquil reservoir routes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to demanding climbs. The routes in Pinares are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from more than 20 reviews. More than 2000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Pinares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Pinares?
A: Pinares offers a diverse and challenging landscape for road cyclists. You'll find a mix of plains, gentle hills, and significant mountain passes like Santa Inés and Montenegro. The region is characterized by extensive pine forests and well-maintained roads, providing varied experiences from speed-focused rides to endurance-testing climbs.

Q: Are there many road cycling routes available in Pinares?
A: Yes, Pinares is a popular destination for road cycling, with over 110 routes available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 7 easy, 62 moderate, and 44 difficult options, ensuring there's something for every cyclist.

Q: What are some of the most challenging road cycling routes in Pinares?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1256296972/montenegro-pena-hincada-and-santa-ines-passes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montenegro, Peña Hincada, and Santa Inés Passes Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain over 100 km. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070846673/rodeo-a-los-picos-de-urbion-circular-por-parque-natural-lagunas-glaciares-de-neila" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rodeo a los Picos de Urbión — circular por Parque Natural Lagunas Glaciares de Neila&lt;/a&gt;, which explores the Picos de Urbión over 122 km.

Q: Are there any moderate road cycling routes that offer scenic views?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070911796/cuerda-del-pozo-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuerda del Pozo Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 45 km route that provides a relaxing ride around the reservoir with beautiful natural surroundings. Another great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070381311/laguna-negra-de-urbion-circular-desde-vinuesa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Negra de Urbión — circular desde Vinuesa&lt;/a&gt;, a 37 km moderate trail through a scenic area.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes in Pinares?
A: Many routes traverse stunning natural landscapes. You can cycle through extensive pine forests, past the Cuerda del Pozo Reservoir, and explore areas like the Sierra de Villafranca and the Parque Natural de Sierra Cebollera. Notable natural attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2516913" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de Urbión&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3328794" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Duero River&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural points of interest accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, some routes combine cycling with sightseeing. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070344737/yacimiento-arqueologico-de-numancia-y-ribera-del-duro-circular-desde-soria" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Yacimiento Arqueológico de Numancia y Ribera del Duro — circular desde Sória&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the archaeological site of Numancia. You might also encounter highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3402819" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of San Saturio&lt;/a&gt; near some routes.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about their experience in Pinares?
A: The road cycling routes in Pinares are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.8 stars from more than 20 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's diverse terrain, well-maintained roads, and the spectacular views encountered along the way.

Q: Is Pinares suitable for family-friendly road cycling?
A: While many routes feature challenging mountain passes, there are also easier and moderate options that can be more suitable for families. Look for routes with lower elevation gain and shorter distances, such as some sections around the Cuerda del Pozo Reservoir, which offer a more relaxed environment.

Q: When is the best time of year to go road cycling in Pinares?
A: Pinares generally enjoys a mild climate for much of the year, making it accessible for outdoor sports without significant seasonal restrictions. Spring and autumn often provide the most comfortable temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer and potential winter snow in higher passes.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Pinares?
A: Many of the popular routes in Pinares are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1256284658/puerto-de-santa-ines-y-puerto-de-piqueras-circular-por-el-parque-natural-de-sierra-cebollera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Santa Inés y Puerto de Piqueras — circular por el Parque Natural de Sierra Cebollera&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1256309113/lagunas-de-neila-from-puerto-el-collado-circular-through-the-parque-natural-de-las-lagunas-de-neila" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lagunas de Neila from Puerto El Collado — circular through the Parque Natural de las Lagunas de Neila&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find routes that take me through the famous pine forests of Pinares?
A: Yes, the region's name 'Pinares' itself signifies the extensive pine forests, and many routes will immerse you in this beautiful environment. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1256318040/southeastern-route-through-the-pinares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Southeastern Route through the Pinares&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 56 km option that specifically highlights this characteristic landscape.
tips: The Roman Catholic Iglesia San Juan de Rabanera, whose construction began in the 12th century, was created in the Romanesque style and has since been renovated several times and expanded with chapels. Due to its detached location at the junction of Calle San Juan de Rabanera and Calle Caballeros, it appears very compact.
It is an old medieval bridge, formerly called the main bridge, built in stone, uniting both banks of the Duero River.
Small town located in the old middle of the Campo de Gómara and some of its houses are bathed by the Río Duero.
Cubo de la Solana is a town that has two parts, the area on the banks of the Río Duero and the west side, where the forests of Cubo de la Solana are located.
On a low hill in the southwest part of the town we find this castle that strategically dominates the surroundings. It is one of the best preserved castles in the province of Soria.
Beautiful road that runs between these two towns in the middle of agricultural fields and a flat area with little gradient.

faqs.question: How challenging is this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular route is rated as moderate for road cycling. It covers nearly 100 km with approximately 608 meters of elevation gain, making it suitable for cyclists with good fitness. It's a substantial ride but generally manageable for experienced road cyclists.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features mostly well-paved surfaces, which are generally easy to ride. However, be prepared for the possibility of some unpaved or more challenging segments, as is common for moderate-difficulty routes in the region. You'll often find relatively low motor vehicle traffic on the communal roads connecting towns like Cubo de la Solana and Almarail.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a blend of natural beauty and historical elements. You'll pass through agricultural fields and areas with gentle gradients. Notable highlights include the San Juan de Rabanera Church, the Medieval Bridge Over the Duero, and the charming settlements of Ribarroya and Cubo de la Solana. You'll also see the Almenar de Soria Castle.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling tour from Soria?
faqs.answer: While the route starts from Soria, specific parking details are not provided. Generally, for routes starting in city centers, it's advisable to look for public parking facilities within Soria city. Consider parking near the city's outskirts or in designated parking areas that allow for longer stays, then cycling to the official start point.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty, nearly 100 km distance, and over 600 meters of elevation gain, this route is generally not recommended for families with young children or beginner cyclists. It's better suited for experienced road cyclists with good fitness levels.

faqs.question: Can I bring my dog on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route primarily on paved surfaces and communal roads. While there are no explicit restrictions against dogs, cycling with a dog on a leash for such a long distance (nearly 100 km) on roads can be challenging and potentially unsafe for both the dog and the cyclist. It's generally not ideal for canine companions.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Soria province experiences warm summers and cold winters. The best times for road cycling are typically spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) when temperatures are milder and more comfortable for a long ride. During these seasons, you can enjoy the scenic agricultural landscapes without extreme heat or cold.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to refuel along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several small towns and settlements, including Cubo de la Solana and Cabrejas del Campo. These villages may offer local cafes or small shops where you can refuel and rest. However, it's always wise to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer stretches between towns.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, this road cycling route uses public roads and communal paths. There are no special permits required, nor are there any entrance fees to access the route or the areas it traverses.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with any other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route intersect with or run alongside several other paths. You'll find it briefly touches upon the Vía Verde Santander-Mediterráneo (Valcorba - Ciria), Ruta del Duero, and the Etapa 04: Ituero – Almazán, among others. These intersections are typically minor portions of the overall route.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling?
faqs.answer: You'll experience a serene rural environment characterized by extensive agricultural fields and generally flat areas with gentle gradients. The region is known for its picturesque views, small Romanesque churches in towns like Rabanera del Campo, and the broader Cubo de la Solana area, which is sometimes described as a 'living outdoor museum' due to its expansive landscapes.

waypoints.name: From Almenar de Soria to Cabrejas del Campo
